(**Argentina U-23** since 1992) represents [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") in international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") competitions during the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") and the [Pan American Games](/wiki/Pan_American_Games "Pan American Games"). The selection is limited to players under the age of 23, except three overage players. The team is controlled by the [Argentine Football Association](/wiki/Argentine_Football_Association "Argentine Football Association") (AFA).

The **Argentina Olympic football team** (**Argentina U-23** since 1992) represents [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") in international [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") competitions during the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") and the [Pan American Games](/wiki/Pan_American_Games "Pan American Games"). The selection is limited to players under the age of 23, except three overage players. The team is controlled by the [Argentine Football Association](/wiki/Argentine_Football_Association "Argentine Football Association") (AFA).

The first participation of Argentina in Olympic tournaments was in [1928](/wiki/Football_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1928 Summer Olympics"), when the team was runner-up to champions [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_under-23_football_team "Uruguay national under-23 football team") at the Games held in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"). By those times, rules stated that only amateur squads could compete,[[1]](#cite_note-afa-1)[[2]](#cite_note-as-2) so Argentina (and also Uruguay) played with senior players so football was still not professional in those countries by then.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Argentina would not participate in Olympic Games until [1960](/wiki/Football_at_the_1960_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics") when the squad had a modest performance, finishing 7th. The team contested the competition with youth amateur players. After the [IOC](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee") allowed professional players to participate (with an age limit of 23 years old) Argentina returned in [1996](/wiki/Football_at_the_1996_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 1996 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament") when the squad won their second silver medal after losing to [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria_national_under-23_football_team "Nigeria national under-23 football team") in the final. In [2004](/wiki/Football_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2004 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament") and coached by [Marcelo Bielsa](/wiki/Marcelo_Bielsa "Marcelo Bielsa"), Argentina won their first gold medal with [Carlos Tevez](/wiki/Carlos_Tevez "Carlos Tevez") finishing as top scorer with eight goals. [Four years later](/wiki/Football_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2008 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament"), Argentina won their second gold medal in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), taking revenge against Nigeria with a 1–0 win in the [final](/wiki/Football_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament_final "Football at the 2008 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament final").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### First participation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: First participation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Argentina_football_team_Olympics_1928.jpg/250px-Argentina_football_team_Olympics_1928.jpg)](/wiki/File:Argentina_football_team_Olympics_1928.jpg)

The team that won the Silver Medal at the [1928 Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1928 Summer Olympics")

Argentina took part for the first time in the [1928 Olympic Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_1928_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1928 Summer Olympics") held in the Netherlands. Although the Olympics were restricted to amateur teams only, Argentina competed with its senior squad so football was not professional in the country until [1931](/wiki/1931_Argentine_Primera_Divisi%C3%B3n "1931 Argentine Primera División").[[1]](#cite_note-afa-1) The team advanced to the final after defeating the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") by a thrashing 11–2 in the first round, and [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium_national_football_team "Belgium national football team") (6–3) in the second. In the semi-finals, the national team smashed [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_national_football_team "Egypt national football team") by 6–0 to qualify for the final against [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay_national_football_team "Uruguay national football team").

The first match ended in a 1–1 tie so a second game had to be played three days later. In the decisive match, Uruguay won the tournament after defeating Argentina 2–1, winning the Gold Medal. The Argentine line-up was [Bossio](/wiki/Angel_Bossio "Angel Bossio"), [Bidoglio](/wiki/Ludovico_Bidoglio "Ludovico Bidoglio"), Paternóster, Médice, [Monti](/wiki/Luis_Monti "Luis Monti"), [Evaristo](/wiki/Juan_Evaristo "Juan Evaristo"), Carricaberri, [Tarasconi](/wiki/Domingo_Tarasconi "Domingo Tarasconi"), [Ferreira](/wiki/Manuel_Ferreira_(footballer) "Manuel Ferreira (footballer)"), Perduca, [Orsi](/wiki/Raimundo_Orsi "Raimundo Orsi"). Tarasconi was also the topscorer of the competition with 11 goals.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### 1932–84: few participations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1932–84: few participations")]

In [1932](/wiki/1932_Summer_Olympics "1932 Summer Olympics") no football tournament was held, and the resumption of the competition in [1936](/wiki/Football_at_the_1936_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1936 Summer Olympics") (where Argentina did not take part) was followed by interruption until [1948](/wiki/Football_at_the_1948_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1948 Summer Olympics") due to [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). Because of an agreement between [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and the [IOC](/wiki/International_Olympic_Committee "International Olympic Committee"), only amateur players were allowed to play in the football tournaments from then on.

Argentina returned to football competition in the [1960](/wiki/Football_at_the_1960_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1960 Summer Olympics") games held in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). The squad was eliminated in the first round after a 3–2 loss to [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark_national_football_team "Denmark national football team"), although the team won its successive games against [Tunisia](/wiki/Tunisia_national_football_team "Tunisia national football team") (2–1) and [Poland](/wiki/Poland_national_football_team "Poland national football team") (2–0). Argentina placed second to Denmark.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Argentina's next participation was at the [1964 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1964_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1964 Summer Olympics") organized by [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), where the team finished in the last position of the group after a 1–1 draw with [Ghana](/wiki/Ghana_national_football_team "Ghana national football team") and a 2–3 loss to [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Since then, Argentina had a long absence from the games, not having taken part in the [1968](/wiki/Football_at_the_1968_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1968 Summer Olympics"), [1972](/wiki/Football_at_the_1972_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1972 Summer Olympics"), [1976](/wiki/Football_at_the_1976_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1976 Summer Olympics"), [1980](/wiki/Football_at_the_1980_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1980 Summer Olympics") and [1984](/wiki/Football_at_the_1984_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1984 Summer Olympics") Olympics.

### 1988–92

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1988–92")]

The national team returned for the [1988 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1988_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1988 Summer Olympics") held in [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"). The changes made by the IOC since [1984](/wiki/Football_at_the_1984_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1984 Summer Olympics") (where Argentina did not participate) allowed the squad to include professional players in their lists,[[2]](#cite_note-as-2) some of them with several years playing in [Primera División](/wiki/Argentine_Primera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Argentine Primera División"), such as [Luis Islas](/wiki/Luis_Islas "Luis Islas"), [Pedro Monzón](/wiki/Pedro_Monz%C3%B3n "Pedro Monzón"), [Néstor Fabbri](/wiki/N%C3%A9stor_Fabbri "Néstor Fabbri"), [Darío Siviski](/wiki/Dar%C3%ADo_Siviski "Darío Siviski") and [Jorge Comas](/wiki/Jorge_Comas_(footballer) "Jorge Comas (footballer)"), among others. In the group stage, Argentina tied 1–1 to the United States, then beat [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team") by 2–1, finishing second to the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union_national_football_team "Soviet Union national football team") and qualifying to the next stage. In the quarter-finals, Argentina lost to Brazil 2–1, being eliminated from the competition.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Since the [1992 edition](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"), the IOC stated that all football players should be under 23 years old, beyond they were professional or not. Coached by [Alfio Basile](/wiki/Alfio_Basile "Alfio Basile"), Argentina went to play the qualification tournament with experienced players such as [Diego Simeone](/wiki/Diego_Simeone "Diego Simeone"), [Diego Latorre](/wiki/Diego_Latorre "Diego Latorre"), [Antonio Mohamed](/wiki/Antonio_Mohamed "Antonio Mohamed"), [Fernando Gamboa](/wiki/Fernando_Gamboa "Fernando Gamboa") and [Leonardo Astrada](/wiki/Leonardo_Astrada "Leonardo Astrada"), who had also won the [Copa América](/wiki/Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "Copa América") [one year before](/wiki/1991_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "1991 Copa América").[[8]](#cite_note-desilusiones-8) Nevertheless, Argentina failed to [qualify](/wiki/1992_CONMEBOL_Pre-Olympic_Tournament "1992 CONMEBOL Pre-Olympic Tournament") for the games, finishing 3rd. in group B after [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_under-23_football_team "Paraguay national under-23 football team") and [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia_national_under-23_football_team "Colombia national under-23 football team") therefore being eliminated in first round.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Since the [1996 Games](/wiki/1996_Summer_Olympics "1996 Summer Olympics"), the IOC allowed squads to include a maximum of three over-23 players in their rosters.[[2]](#cite_note-as-2)[[10]](#cite_note-mem-10)

### Return to podium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Return to podium")]

Argentina came back to the competition in the [1996](/wiki/Football_at_the_1996_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1996 Summer Olympics") edition held in [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"), United States. For the first time in the history of the Olympics, the IOC allowed football representatives to register a maximum of three above-23 players. The Argentine players registered under that condition were [Diego Simeone](/wiki/Diego_Simeone "Diego Simeone"), [José Chamot](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Chamot "José Chamot") and [Roberto Sensini](/wiki/Roberto_Sensini "Roberto Sensini"). Former [senior team](/wiki/Argentina_national_football_team "Argentina national football team") captain [Daniel Passarella](/wiki/Daniel_Passarella "Daniel Passarella") was the manager.

The national team debuted with a 3–1 victory over the United States, then tied to [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_football_team "Portugal national football team") and Tunisia, both 1–1, to finish first the group and qualify for the second round. In the quarter-finals, Argentina trashed [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team") 4–0 which allowed the team to pass to the semi-finals, where it defeated Portugal 2–0. After 66 years since the first final played in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), Argentina reached its second Olympic final. The match was played on 3 August 1996 and Argentina lost to [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria_national_football_team "Nigeria national football team") 2–3.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The line-up for the final was: [Cavallero](/wiki/Pablo_Cavallero "Pablo Cavallero"); [Javier Zanetti](/wiki/Javier_Zanetti "Javier Zanetti"), [Roberto Ayala](/wiki/Roberto_Ayala "Roberto Ayala"), [Roberto Sensini](/wiki/Roberto_Sensini "Roberto Sensini"), [José Chamot](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Chamot "José Chamot"); [Christian Bassedas](/wiki/Christian_Bassedas "Christian Bassedas"), [Matías Almeyda](/wiki/Mat%C3%ADas_Almeyda "Matías Almeyda"), [Ariel Ortega](/wiki/Ariel_Ortega "Ariel Ortega"), [Hugo Morales](/wiki/Hugo_Morales_(footballer) "Hugo Morales (footballer)"); [Claudio López](/wiki/Claudio_L%C3%B3pez_(footballer) "Claudio López (footballer)") and [Hernán Crespo](/wiki/Hern%C3%A1n_Crespo "Hernán Crespo"). Other players squad players included [Carlos Bossio](/wiki/Carlos_Bossio "Carlos Bossio"), [Marcelo Gallardo](/wiki/Marcelo_Gallardo "Marcelo Gallardo") and [Marcelo Delgado](/wiki/Marcelo_Delgado "Marcelo Delgado").[[12]](#cite_note-12) In the next edition of the Games, [2000](/wiki/2000_Summer_Olympics "2000 Summer Olympics"), Argentina did not participate.

### First gold

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: First gold")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Carlos_Tevez_with_Argentina_at_the_Boleyn_Ground_in_November_2014.jpg/120px-Carlos_Tevez_with_Argentina_at_the_Boleyn_Ground_in_November_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carlos_Tevez_with_Argentina_at_the_Boleyn_Ground_in_November_2014.jpg)

[Carlos Tevez](/wiki/Carlos_Tevez "Carlos Tevez"), key player and top scorer (8 goals) in 2004, when Argentina won its first gold medal

The [2004 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_2004_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 2004 Summer Olympics") were held in [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") and Argentina returned to the competition after the absence in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"). The squad, managed by [Marcelo Bielsa](/wiki/Marcelo_Bielsa "Marcelo Bielsa"), won the gold medal for the first time in its history. Before playing the final, Argentina won all the games in the first round, thrashing [Serbia and Montenegro](/wiki/Serbia_national_football_team "Serbia national football team") 6–0 then defeating Tunisia and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team"). Argentina finished first in the group with no goals conceded. In the quarter-finals, Argentina smashed [Costa Rica](/wiki/Costa_Rica_national_football_team "Costa Rica national football team") 4–0, reaching the semi-finals against [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_football_team "Italy national football team") which it beat 3–0. Argentina played the final against [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_football_team "Paraguay national football team") on 28 August 2004, winning not only the game (1–0) but the gold medal as well.

Argentina won the competition with an astounding campaign, winning the six matches played, with no goals allowed during the tournament. The team also totaled 17 goals (2.83 per match). The line-up for the final was: [Germán Lux](/wiki/Germ%C3%A1n_Lux "Germán Lux"); [Fabricio Coloccini](/wiki/Fabricio_Coloccini "Fabricio Coloccini"), [Roberto Ayala](/wiki/Roberto_Ayala "Roberto Ayala"), [Gabriel Heinze](/wiki/Gabriel_Heinze "Gabriel Heinze"); [Lucho González](/wiki/Lucho_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Lucho González"), [Javier Mascherano](/wiki/Javier_Mascherano "Javier Mascherano"), [Kily González](/wiki/Kily_Gonz%C3%A1lez "Kily González"), [Andrés D'Alessandro](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9s_D%27Alessandro "Andrés D'Alessandro"), [Carlos Tevez](/wiki/Carlos_Tevez "Carlos Tevez"); [Mauro Rosales](/wiki/Mauro_Rosales "Mauro Rosales") and [César Delgado](/wiki/C%C3%A9sar_Delgado "César Delgado"). The most notable player of the tournament was Tevez, who finished as topscorer with eight goals.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Second gold

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Second gold")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Messi_olympics-soccer-7.jpg/250px-Messi_olympics-soccer-7.jpg)](/wiki/File:Messi_olympics-soccer-7.jpg)

[Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi") against Brazil in the semi-final match in 2008, when the team won its second gold medal

The [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 2008 Summer Olympics") were held in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing") where Argentina, coached by former World Champion [Sergio Batista](/wiki/Sergio_Batista "Sergio Batista"), won their second consecutive gold medal. The squad debuted with a 2–1 victory over the [Ivory Coast](/wiki/Ivory_Coast_national_under-23_football_team "Ivory Coast national under-23 football team"), then defeating [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_under-23_soccer_team "Australia men's national under-23 soccer team") (1–0) and [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia_national_under-23_football_team "Serbia national under-23 football team") (2–0). In the knockout stage, Argentina eliminated the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_under-21_football_team "Netherlands national under-21 football team") ([aet](/wiki/Overtime_(sports)#Knock-out_contests_.28including_professional_competition.29 "Overtime (sports)")) by 2–1, thrashed [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_under-23_football_team "Brazil national under-23 football team") by 3–0 and won the gold medal in the final match against [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria_national_under-23_football_team "Nigeria national under-23 football team"), 1–0.

Argentina won all the matches played (six), scoring 11 goals with only two conceded. Some of the most notable players of the tournament were [Lionel Messi](/wiki/Lionel_Messi "Lionel Messi"), [Sergio Agüero](/wiki/Sergio_Ag%C3%BCero "Sergio Agüero"), [Ángel Di María](/wiki/%C3%81ngel_Di_Mar%C3%ADa "Ángel Di María"), [Éver Banega](/wiki/%C3%89ver_Banega "Éver Banega"), [Ezequiel Lavezzi](/wiki/Ezequiel_Lavezzi "Ezequiel Lavezzi"), [Fernando Gago](/wiki/Fernando_Gago "Fernando Gago") and [Pablo Zabaleta](/wiki/Pablo_Zabaleta "Pablo Zabaleta"), who would all play for the senior team in successive years.

The three over-23 years players were [Juan Román Riquelme](/wiki/Juan_Rom%C3%A1n_Riquelme "Juan Román Riquelme"), Javier Mascherano and [Nicolás Pareja](/wiki/Nicol%C3%A1s_Pareja "Nicolás Pareja").

### 2012–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Argentina_national_under-23_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2012–present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Argentina_x_Honduras_-_Futebol_masculino_-_Olimp%C3%ADadas_Rio_2016_%2828896601585%29.jpg/250px-Argentina_x_Honduras_-_Futebol_masculino_-_Olimp%C3%ADadas_Rio_2016_%2828896601585%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Argentina_x_Honduras_-_Futebol_masculino_-_Olimp%C3%ADadas_Rio_2016_(28896601585).jpg)

[Anthony Lozano](/wiki/Anthony_Lozano "Anthony Lozano") scores the goal for Honduras during the match where Argentina was eliminated in 2016

Argentina failed to qualify for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 2012 Summer Olympics") held in [London](/wiki/London "London"). The [2011 South American U-20 Championship](/wiki/2011_South_American_U-20_Championship "2011 South American U-20 Championship") qualified the top two teams for the Olympics. Argentina failed to qualify in the final stage, finishing 3rd. after Brazil and Uruguay.

For the [2016](/wiki/Football_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 2016 Summer Olympics") competition held in [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), most of the players called up for the squad were not given permission to play by their respective clubs, including [Paulo Dybala](/wiki/Paulo_Dybala "Paulo Dybala"), [Mauro Icardi](/wiki/Mauro_Icardi "Mauro Icardi"), [Matías Kranevitter](/wiki/Mat%C3%ADas_Kranevitter "Matías Kranevitter"), [Luciano Vietto](/wiki/Luciano_Vietto "Luciano Vietto"), [Ramiro Funes Mori](/wiki/Ramiro_Funes_Mori "Ramiro Funes Mori") and goalkeeper [Augusto Batalla](/wiki/Augusto_Batalla "Augusto Batalla"), among others.[[15]](#cite_note-15) After the resignation of [Gerardo Martino](/wiki/Gerardo_Martino "Gerardo Martino") as coach, [Julio Olarticoechea](/wiki/Julio_Olarticoechea "Julio Olarticoechea") (who was the [Argentina U-20](/wiki/Argentina_national_under-20_football_team "Argentina national under-20 football team") coach) was appointed to take over the team.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

At [Rio 2016](/wiki/Football_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament"), the squad debuted with a 2–0 loss to [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal_national_under-21_football_team "Portugal national under-21 football team"), then defeating [Algeria](/wiki/Algeria_national_under-23_football_team "Algeria national under-23 football team") 2–1. In the last fixture of group stage, Argentina drew 1–1 with [Honduras](/wiki/Honduras_national_under-23_football_team "Honduras national under-23 football team"), which caused the squad finished third in the group, not enough to qualify for the next round.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Some of Argentina's players were [Ángel Correa](/wiki/%C3%81ngel_Correa "Ángel Correa"), [Jonathan Calleri](/wiki/Jonathan_Calleri "Jonathan Calleri") and [Cristian Pavón](/wiki/Cristian_Pav%C3%B3n "Cristian Pavón").

In [Tokyo 2020](/wiki/Football_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2020 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament"), Argentina debuted in group C with a 2–0 loss to [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_under-23_soccer_team "Australia men's national under-23 soccer team"), then beating [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_national_under-23_football_team "Egypt national under-23 football team") 1–0. The team tied 1–1 to [Spain](/wiki/Spain_national_under-23_football_team "Spain national under-23 football team"), finishing third in the group and failing to qualify to the next stage. [Fernando Batista](/wiki/Fernando_Batista "Fernando Batista") was the head coach. Like the previous edition in Rio, several clubs denied their players to play for Argentina, some examples were [Gonzalo Montiel](/wiki/Gonzalo_Montiel "Gonzalo Montiel"), Cristian Romero, Exequiel Palacios, [Lautaro Martínez](/wiki/Lautaro_Mart%C3%ADnez "Lautaro Martínez"), [Julián Álvarez](/wiki/Juli%C3%A1n_%C3%81lvarez_(footballer) "Julián Álvarez (footballer)"), Lisandro Martínez, Nicolás Domínguez, Nicolás González, and Nahuel Molina (went on vacation after playing the [2019 Copa América](/wiki/2020_Copa_Am%C3%A9rica "2020 Copa América")); on the other hand, footballers playing for teams outside Argentine were not also allowed to play, such as [Matías Zaracho](/wiki/Mat%C3%ADas_Zaracho "Matías Zaracho"), Nicolás Capaldo, [Juan Foyth](/wiki/Juan_Foyth "Juan Foyth"), Marcos Senesi, and [Leonardo Balerdi](/wiki/Leonardo_Balerdi "Leonardo Balerdi"). The large list of players denied also included over-23 players [Carlos Izquierdoz](/wiki/Carlos_Izquierdoz "Carlos Izquierdoz"), [Enzo Pérez](/wiki/Enzo_P%C3%A9rez "Enzo Pérez"), [Ángel Correa](/wiki/%C3%81ngel_Correa "Ángel Correa"), [Nacho Fernández](/wiki/Nacho_Fern%C3%A1ndez "Nacho Fernández"), [Sebastián Driussi](/wiki/Sebasti%C3%A1n_Driussi "Sebastián Driussi"), [Agustín Marchesín](/wiki/Agust%C3%ADn_Marches%C3%ADn "Agustín Marchesín"), and [Juan Musso](/wiki/Juan_Musso "Juan Musso").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Rules

* 1900–1904: *club teams*[[1]](#cite_note-afa-1)[[2]](#cite_note-as-2)
* 1908–1984: *amateur / youth national teams*[[n1 1]](#cite_note-fifaint-19)[[n1 2]](#cite_note-eastern-20)
* 1988: *professional players who had not played in [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup")*[[19]](#cite_note-21)
* 1992: *under-23 national teams*[[2]](#cite_note-as-2)[[10]](#cite_note-mem-10)
* 1996–present: *under-23 national teams (with three 'no age limit' players allowed, after an agreement between [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") and [OIC](/wiki/OIC "OIC"))*[[2]](#cite_note-as-2)[[10]](#cite_note-mem-10)
